title: Represent the commutative property of multiplication
description: Use arrays, equal groups, and number lines to visualize the commutative property of multiplication.
topic_path: Get ready for equations, expressions, and inequalities
Represent the commutative property of multiplication

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which two expressions represent the following array?**

![An array with 3 rows with 6 dots per row](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/6cd1441393d3e6365da4e2f67befa272d24dbafa)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
There are $\blueD{3}$ rows with $\purpleC{6}$ dots each.

![The same array with the rows numbered 1 through 3 and the dots in the row numbered 1 through 6](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a384a315ea85aa0357cf16188175fd0b8b77d381)

The expression $\blueD{3} \times \purpleC{6}$ represents the array. There are $18$ total dots.

We can group the same array of $18$ dots into columns instead of rows.

There are $\purpleC{6}$ columns with $\blueD{3}$ dots each.

![The same array with the columns numbered 1 through 6 and the dots in the column numbered 1 through 3](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/06cf42dc09c9ca470600dcaf2df9f9847edd368c)

The expression $\purpleC{6} \times \blueD{3}$ also represents the array.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
We can represent the array with the expressions $\blueD{3} \times \purpleC{6}$ and $\purpleC{6} \times \blueD{3}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which two expressions represent the following number line?**

![A number line labeled 0, 5, 10, 15, 20. Arrows point right from each number to the next.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ef9583eb047cccb0a69aef2b5bff214b3914fe36)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
There are $\maroonD{4}$ jumps of $\blueD{5}$ units each.

![The same number line with each arrow labeled plus 5. A brace labeled, 4 equal jumps, spans all 4 arrows](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/0609c04fcf4bf1d8e607a4393205947868638814)

The expression $\maroonD{4} \times \blueD{5}$ represents the number line.

We can reach the same total, $20$, using $\blueD{5}$ jumps of $\maroonD{4}$ units each.

![A number line labeled 0, 4, 8, 12, 16, 20. Arrows labeled, plus 4, point right from each number to the next. A brace labeled, 5 equal jumps, spans all 5 arrows.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/ce31241e106c7612ed7a6adffaa8f342b9e07218)

The expression $\blueD{5} \times \maroonD{4}$ is equivalent to $\maroonD{4} \times \blueD{5}$.

[[☃ explanation 2]]
We can represent the number line with the expressions $\blueD{5} \times \maroonD{4}$ and $\maroonD{4} \times \blueD{5}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which two expressions represent the following picture?**

![9 groups of 7 suns](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a9cfce2497935c5c188e373442f2dc8e89bf5836)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
There are $\blueD{9}$ groups with $\maroonD{7}$ suns each.

![9 groups of 7 suns](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a9cfce2497935c5c188e373442f2dc8e89bf5836)

The expression $\blueD{9} \times \maroonD{7}$ represents the picture.

There are $63$ total suns.

We can rearrange those same $63$ suns to show $\maroonD{7}$ groups with $\blueD{9}$ suns in each.

![7 groups of 9 suns](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/730ec6d33bc65d8019977d25243af35ae8b6ae2f)

The expression $\maroonD{7} \times \blueD{9}$ also represents the picture.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
We can represent the picture with the expressions $\blueD{9} \times \maroonD{7}$ and $\maroonD{7} \times \blueD{9}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which two expressions represent the following picture?**

![8 groups of 2 triangles](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8c5f479c236d877bec243597867a3be9d43d45cf)

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints:
There are $\blueD{8}$ groups with $\maroonD{2}$ triangles each.

![8 groups of 2 triangles](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8c5f479c236d877bec243597867a3be9d43d45cf)

The expression $\blueD{8} \times \maroonD{2}$ represents the picture.

There are $16$ total triangles.

We can rearrange those same $16$ triangles to show $\maroonD{2}$ groups with $\blueD{8}$ triangles in each.

![2 groups of 8 triangles](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/97de0741c521e66fbf9905af16331426fe2b91d9)

The expression $\maroonD{2} \times \blueD{8}$ also represents the picture.

[[☃ explanation 1]]
We can represent the picture with the expressions $\blueD{8} \times \maroonD{2}$ and $\maroonD{2} \times \blueD{8}$.

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which $2$ pictures could we use to represent $5 \times 2$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: $\tealD{5} \times \goldE{2}$ can mean $\tealD{5}$ groups of $\goldE{2}$.

This picture shows $\tealD{5}$ groups with $\goldE{2}$ planes in each.

![5 groups of 2 planes](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/18e23559ad3581629f34cacc28020576e334c44b)

There are $10$ total planes.

We can rearrange those same $10$ planes to show $\goldE{2}$ groups with $\tealD{5}$ planes in each.

![2 groups of 5 planes](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/df6997facb9fc4f667ca618ef71f2f8cb3309d3d)

[[☃ explanation 1]]

These pictures could represent $5\times 2$:

![5 groups of 2 planes](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/18e23559ad3581629f34cacc28020576e334c44b)

![2 groups of 5 planes](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7087c9f4ecf105fd0f574e1b303c348a7ab1199b)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which $2$ number lines could we use to represent $9 \times 5$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: $\tealD{9} \times \goldE{5}$ can mean $\tealD{9}$ groups of $\goldE{5}$.

This number line shows $\tealD{9}$ jumps of $\goldE{5}$ units each.

![A number line labeled 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45. Arrows labeled, plus 5, point right from each number to the next. A brace labeled, 9 equal jumps, spans all 9 arrows.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/8b3183c1d3750d448ceed612e88ce350fdc3dcaa)

It starts at $0$ and ends at $45$.

We can also reach $45$ using $\goldE{5}$ jumps of $\tealD{9}$ units each.

![A number line labeled 0, 9, 18, 27, 36, 45. Arrows labeled, plus 9, point right from each number to the next. A brace labeled, 5 equal jumps, spans all 5 arrows.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7fe2433423618920574a0098ecb364c83d82f5b3)

[[☃ explanation 1]]

These pictures could represent $9\times 5$:

![A number line labeled 0, 5, 10, 15, 20, 25, 30, 35, 40, 45. Arrows point right from each number to the next.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/873441367ebd0c7347a1fa0f98e81e27a2abd14b)

![A number line labeled 0, 9, 18, 27, 36, 45. Arrows point right from each number to the next.](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d4a1c2ce1adbff75e479d14bd6a5578a13220c10)

assessment_items.question_content:
**Which $2$ pictures could we use to represent $4 \times 8$ ?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: $\blueD{4} \times \purpleC{8}$ can mean $\blueD{4}$ groups of $\purpleC{8}$.

This picture shows $\blueD{4}$ rows with $\purpleC{8}$ acorns in each.

![4 rows of 8 acorns, with lines between each row. The rows are numbered 1 through 4. The acorns in the row are numbered 1 through 8](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f98f506516ffd395b18ace3ec69565e61a513893)

There are $32$ total acorns.

We can rearrange those same $32$ acorns to show $\purpleC{8}$ rows with $\blueD{4}$ acorns in each.

![8 rows of 4 acorns, with lines between each column. The columns are numbered 1 through 4. The acorns in the column are numbered 1 through 8](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/911ac448b370edb1cfacd9a66d74a1350a6abe39)

[[☃ explanation 1]]

These pictures could represent $4\times 8$:

![4 rows of 8 acorns](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/068fe7ac5c284954b87c4d0219f0dd4b5c574f64)

![8 rows of 4 acorns](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/4515e2353feb274922b358323315d84739518d7c)

choices.content: ![A row of 8 acorns and a row of 4 acorns](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/98f58456bc5465d340e8b1b7d05a8017c2179932)

assessment_items.question_content:
Jen has $21$ toy cars.

**What are two ways Jen can group her cars?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's try each set of groups to see whether it equals $21$ cars.

###$3$ groups of $7$ cars

![3 groups of 7 cars](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/98d58ae50df4d4d99b4fd0f3721157b44d35efb8)

$7+7+7=21$ , so Jen can group her cars this way.

###$7$ groups of $3$ cars

![7 groups of 3 cars](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/9fec4cb33f28f50c87a54e548c6de356ed5c6591)

$3+3+3+3+3+3+3=21$ , so Jen can group her cars this way.

###$4$ groups of $5$ cars

![4 groups of 5 cars](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/58386d1250d0cddc0e71a9b22398f3e4791d26a8)

$5+5+5+5=20$ , so Jen **cannot** group her $21$ cars this way.

###$2$ groups of $10$ cars

![2 groups of 10 cars](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/f0d82d4af278e55d27e2204bd92e56214a628faf)

$10+10=20$ , so Jen **cannot** group her $21$ cars this way.

Jen can groups her cars these ways:

- $3$ groups of $7$ cars
- $7$ groups of $3$ cars

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Owen has $36$ pencils.

**What are two ways Owen can group his pencils?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's try each set of groups to see whether it equals $36$ pencils.

###$3$ groups of $6$ pencils

![3 groups of 6 pencils](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7363b1edb8610a7e049322cb743245b6ea532ad2)

$6+6+6=18$ , so Owen **cannot** group his $36$ pencils this way.

###$9$ groups of $4$ pencils

![9 groups of 4 pencils](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/7f101af798320c330277af5f5ab258455377fc17)

$4+4+4+4+4+4+4+4+4=36$

So Owen can group his pencils this way.

###$6$ groups of $3$ pencils

![6 groups of 3 pencils](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/3dbc08fcfdd5413f466831585c84b092dfdb3965)

$3+3+3+3+3+3=18$ , so Owen **cannot** group his $36$ pencils this way.

###$4$ groups of $9$ pencils

![4 groups of 9 pencils](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/c5359bf543e9f1c4a9bd9395bcf23eb0dac0681c)

$9+9+9+9=36$ , so Owen can group his pencils this way.

Owen can groups his pencils these ways:

- $9$ groups of $4$ pencils
- $4$ groups of $9$ pencils

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Bo has $56$ cards.

**What are two ways Bo can stack her cards?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's try each set of stacks to see whether it equals $56$ cards.

###$6$ stacks of $9$ cards

![6 stacks of 9 cards](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/a9d3e73e3772d3c91430356702b4cf89bdfce9c6)

$9+9+9+9+9+9=54$ , so Bo *cannot* stack her $56$ cards this way.

###$7$ stacks of $8$ cards

![7 stacks of 8 cards](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/017bfcefe0e5143612375361b9a99ef72eb67a8b)

$8+8+8+8+8+8+8=56$ , so Bo can stack her cards this way.

###$8$ stacks of $7$ cards

![8 stacks of 7 cards](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/b19d65a804c302b93d61c05a73b22af1a37d2cf5)

$7+7+7+7+7+7+7+7=56$ , so Bo can stack her cards this way.

###$9$ stacks of $6$ cards

![9 stacks of 6 cards](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/d287c94c1bc0cf84dc478708052461205983af9e)

$6+6+6+6+6+6+6+6+6=54$

So Bo *cannot* stack her $56$ cards this way.

Bo can stack her cards these ways:

- $7$ stacks of $8$ cards
- $8$ stacks of $7$ cards

[[☃ explanation 1]]

assessment_items.question_content:
Vicky has $12$ plates.

**What are two ways Vicky can stack their plates?**

[[☃ radio 1]]
hints: Let's try each set of stacks to see whether it equals $12$ plates.

###$10$ stacks of $2$ plates

![10 stacks of 2 plates](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/e236caf6ae252b7e4878874e24494b9ab86f2e17)

$2+2+2+2+2+2+2+2+2+2=20$

So Vicky *cannot* stack their $12$ plates this way.

###$8$ stacks of $4$ plates

![8 stacks of 4 plates](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/61fc82758935689a75573d3e99068b7eebacb76b)

$4+4+4+4+4+4+4+4=32$

So Vicky *cannot* stack their $12$ plates this way.

###$2$ stacks of $6$ plates

![2 stacks of 6 plates](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/2acc638135d60611bcc615db06a655a6e3233dee)

$6+6=12$ , so Vicky can stack their plates this way.

###$6$ stacks of $2$ plates

![6 stacks of 2 plates](web+graphie:${☣ LOCALPATH}/images/11de40461574c26359055b1cc7b015c9311f15ff)

$2+2+2+2+2+2=12$ , so Vicky can stack their plates this way.

Vicky can stack their plates these ways:

- $2$ stacks of $6$ plates 
- $6$ stacks of $2$ plates

[[☃ explanation 1]]
